_note: Hand-checked authorities for the worked demo case. Matched to an issue by the 'when' keyword. Real cases for true propositions; Nigeria is flagged for confirmation rather than invented (thin open-data coverage).

authorities.name: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd
authorities.point: Whether a breach allows termination turns on whether it deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract (the innominate term approach).
authorities.turning_point: The court refused a rigid condition/warranty split and asked how serious the breach's consequences were; the delay did not go to the root, so termination was not justified.

authorities.name: Bunge Corporation v Tradax Export SA
authorities.point: In mercantile contracts, stipulations as to time are generally conditions, so late performance of a time term can entitle the innocent party to terminate.
authorities.turning_point: The need for certainty in commercial dealings led the House of Lords to treat the time stipulation as a condition, allowing termination.

authorities.name: Ramirez v. Autosport
authorities.point: Under the UCC perfect tender rule (s 2-601), a buyer may reject goods that fail to conform in any respect, subject to the seller's right to cure.
authorities.turning_point: Rejection was upheld because the seller failed to cure within a reasonable time, not merely because the tender was imperfect.

authorities.name: Channel Island Ferries Ltd v Sealink UK Ltd
authorities.point: A 'beyond reasonable control' force majeure clause requires the party to show it took all reasonable steps to avoid or mitigate the event.
authorities.turning_point: Reliance on the clause failed because the party had not shown it took reasonable steps; the event alone was not enough.

authorities.name: Kel Kim Corp. v. Central Markets, Inc.
authorities.point: Force majeure clauses are construed narrowly; only events specifically listed in the clause excuse performance.
authorities.turning_point: Because the disabling event was not enumerated in the clause, the court held performance was not excused.

authorities.name: Sale of Goods Act (Nigerian state equivalents of the English 1893 Act)
authorities.cite: Sale of Goods Act 1893 / applicable Nigerian state statute
authorities.point: Nigerian sale-of-goods law largely mirrors the English Sale of Goods Act; force majeure remains a matter of the contract's express terms.
authorities.turning_point: Coverage note: Nigerian case law on force majeure strikes is thin in open data. Confirm the controlling state statute and any Supreme Court authority before relying on this.

authorities.name: Gilbert-Ash (Northern) Ltd v Modern Engineering (Bristol) Ltd
authorities.point: Clear words are needed to exclude a common law right of set-off; absent that, a buyer may withhold against claims for defective performance.
authorities.turning_point: The House of Lords read the contract as preserving set-off because the exclusion was not expressed clearly enough.

authorities.name: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s 53
authorities.point: On breach of warranty the buyer may set up the diminution in value of the goods against the price rather than withhold the whole sum.
authorities.turning_point: The remedy is a price reduction reflecting the defect, which limits how much of the price may properly be withheld.
